diagrams.title: Binary Relations Properties Overview
diagrams.description: A comprehensive diagram showing the four main properties of binary relations (Reflexive, Symmetric, Antisymmetric, Transitive) with directed graph examples on a set {a, b, c}. Each property is illustrated with a specific relation that demonstrates that property.
elements: Four property boxes arranged in 2x2 grid
Each box contains: property name, formal definition, directed graph example
Directed graphs show nodes {a,b,c} with arrows representing ordered pairs
diagrams.purpose: Visualize and compare the four fundamental properties of binary relations with concrete examples, helping students understand how each property manifests in a directed graph representation.

diagrams.title: Equivalence vs Partial Order Relations
diagrams.description: A comparison diagram showing how equivalence relations (reflexive, symmetric, transitive) and partial order relations (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive) are formed. Uses a Venn diagram-style layout showing the property combinations.
elements: Two main circles labeled 'Equivalence Relation' and 'Partial Order Relation'
Three smaller circles inside for reflexive, symmetric/symmetric-like, transitive
Arrow indicators showing which properties combine
Set notation showing: Equivalence = Reflexive ∧ Symmetric ∧ Transitive
Set notation showing: Partial Order = Reflexive ∧ Antisymmetric ∧ Transitive
Example equivalence relation: congruence mod n
Example partial order: ≤ on real numbers
diagrams.purpose: Clarify the difference between equivalence relations and partial orders by showing the exact property combinations required for each, reinforcing the structural differences between these important relation types.

diagrams.title: Hasse Diagram Example
diagrams.description: A Hasse diagram showing a partial order relation on the power set of {a, b}. Demonstrates how antisymmetric and transitive properties create a hierarchical structure that can be represented as a simplified directed graph without transitive edges.
elements: Nodes arranged vertically showing subset inclusion
Note explaining why arrows point upward (smaller to larger sets)
Annotation showing this relation is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive
Label highlighting that this is a poset (partially ordered set)
diagrams.purpose: Introduce Hasse diagrams as a visual tool for representing partial orders, showing how the complexity of relations can be simplified while preserving the essential ordering structure.
